step_by_step_thinking: \boxed{A} Anaphylaxis is indeed a result of mast cell activation. Multiple references describe mast-cell-stabilizing agents like cromolyn and ketotifen, which inhibit histamine and leukotriene release — key mediators in allergic and anaphylactic responses. Mast cells are central to initiating immediate-type hypersensitivity reactions, releasing histamine and other mediators upon activation, leading to vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and bronchoconstriction—all hallmark features of anaphylaxis. Therefore, mast cell activation directly drives the pathophysiology of anaphylaxis.
raw_response:
\boxed{A} Anaphylaxis is indeed a result of mast cell activation. Multiple references describe mast-cell-stabilizing agents like cromolyn and ketotifen, which inhibit histamine and leukotriene release — key mediators in allergic and anaphylactic responses. Mast cells are central to initiating immediate-type hypersensitivity reactions, releasing histamine and other mediators upon activation, leading to vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and bronchoconstriction—all hallmark features of anaphylaxis. Therefore, mast cell activation directly drives the pathophysiology of anaphylaxis.
\boxed{A}
